triage/source-descriptors.name: Source Descriptors (Triage Pool)
triage/source-descriptors.description: Per-source lifecycle declarations driving TTL and quarantine triggers in the daily pool sweep

content.full:
# Source Descriptors

Every entry in the triage pool was captured from some **source** ŌĆö a journal
thread, a Chrome tab, an inline Obsidian selection, an email message, etc.
Sources differ in lifecycle expectations:

- A journal thread might be re-edited by the user as the day's running notes
  evolve; the original captured text might no longer reflect the user's intent.
- A Chrome tab can be closed; once gone, the captured page text is a ghost.
- An inline `send-to-agent` selection is the user's affirmative capture;
  treating it as transient would erode trust (V2a ŌĆö capture promise integrity).
- An email moved to Trash / Archive / a custom folder is no longer in the
  inbox view the user would act from; the bridge confirms the move via
  `POST /messages/exists`.

Rather than hard-coding per-source TTLs and quarantine rules in the sweeper,
each source declares a `SourceDescriptor`. The daily sweep dispatches on the
descriptor; new sources register their own descriptor and inherit the sweep
machinery for free.

## Anatomy of a descriptor

```python
SourceDescriptor(
    name="journal_thread",
    ttl_days=5,
    quarantine_triggers=[
        "source_removed",
        "source_edited_beyond_match",
    ],
    config={
        "edit_match_threshold": 0.85,
    },
)
```

- `name` ŌĆö the source identifier (matches `PoolEntry.source`).
- `ttl_days` ŌĆö soft expiry in days, or `None` for no auto-expiry. After this
  many days, the sweep transitions `state` from `pending` to `stale`.
- `quarantine_triggers` ŌĆö ordered list of trigger names. The sweep evaluates
  each in order; the first one that fires sets `quarantine_reason` and
  transitions `state` to `quarantined`.
- `config` ŌĆö open dict of source-specific knobs the trigger functions read
  (e.g. inline's `capture_tag`, journal's `edit_match_threshold`, email's
  `open_action`).

## Defaults shipped

| Source            | TTL  | Triggers                                           |
|-------------------|------|----------------------------------------------------|
| `journal_thread`  | 5d   | `source_removed`, `source_edited_beyond_match`     |
| `chrome_tab`      | 2d   | `source_removed`                                   |
| `inline`          | null | `source_removed`                                   |
| `email_message`   | 7d   | `source_removed`                                   |

Inline TTL is intentionally `null`. User-affirmative captures should not
auto-expire.

## Override path

Users override per-source under `triage.pool.sources.<name>` in
`config.local.yaml`. Deep-merges on top of code defaults; missing keys
inherit:

```yaml
triage:
  pool:
    sources:
      journal_thread:
        ttl_days: 14   # extend journal TTL; everything else inherits
      inline:
        config:
          capture_tag: "wb/saved"   # override one config key, others inherit
```

New sources register a fresh descriptor (any keys not specified default to
empty / null).

## Trigger functions

Live in `work_buddy/triage/sources_triggers.py`. Dispatched by name from
the descriptor's `quarantine_triggers` list. Contract:

```python
def trigger_<name>(
    entry: PoolEntry,
    descriptor: SourceDescriptor,
) -> str | None
```

Return `None` for "did not fire"; return a non-empty string for the
human-readable reason that gets persisted to `quarantine_reason`.

**Triggers MUST be defensive.** The sweep runs daily, unattended, against
every pending entry. A trigger that raises kills the whole pass. Catch
transport / filesystem failures and return `None` (treat ambiguous as
"still live"); never raise.

Currently shipped:

- `source_removed` ŌĆö generic "the captured source no longer resolves."
  Per-source resolution lives inside the trigger:
  - `inline`: file at `metadata.file_path` no longer exists
  - `journal_thread`: journal file for the source dates no longer exists
  - `chrome_tab`: tab id absent from the live tab ledger
  - `email_message`: bridge `provider.message_exists()` returns False
    (message moved out of its captured folder URI or deleted; bridge
    ambiguity returns None and never quarantines)
- `source_edited_beyond_match` ŌĆö captured text no longer found in source
  (difflib.SequenceMatcher.ratio < threshold; default 0.85).
- `tag_removed` ŌĆö placeholder for the future inline tag-write flow.
  Currently a no-op.

## Per-source open-in-app actions

Descriptors may also carry an `open_action` config block (read by the
Review UI, not the sweep). See `triage/card-actions` for the shape and
contract ŌĆö used by `email_message` to declare the
"Open in Thunderbird" button + auto-quarantine on `email_message_not_found`.

## Per-entry quarantine without a sweep

`triage_pool_quarantine_entry(run_id, item_id, reason)` is the
lightweight counterpart to `triage_pool_sweep` when the caller already
knows a specific entry is stale. The dashboard uses it to self-heal
Review cards on action-click failure (see `triage/card-actions`).
Idempotent ŌĆö re-running on an already-quarantined entry returns
`stamped: 0`.

## Why this lives separate from `triage/config.py`

The config file holds *stage-shape* config ŌĆö profile names, max_tokens,
the escalation chain. Knobs about HOW the LLM call runs.

The source registry holds *lifecycle* config ŌĆö TTLs, quarantine triggers.
Knobs about how entries AGE in the pool.

Different concerns, different override surfaces, different consumers.
Keeping them in separate files lets the migration script and the daily
sweep import the lifecycle config without pulling in the LLM stack.

triage/source-descriptors.dev_notes:
Naming: descriptor data lives in `sources.py`; trigger functions in `sources_triggers.py`. Keep these split ŌĆö `sources.py` is data-only and importable from migration scripts without paying for bridge / vault helpers. Importing it must NOT pull in obsidian.bridge.

Adding a trigger: append the name to `KNOWN_TRIGGERS` in `sources.py`, write the function in `sources_triggers.py` with signature `(entry, descriptor) -> str | None`, register in `_TRIGGER_DISPATCH`. Triggers MUST be defensive ŌĆö return None on any failure (transport, ambiguity); never raise. The sweep is unattended, one bad trigger poisons the whole pass otherwise.

Descriptors validate at construction: unknown trigger names raise. This is intentional ŌĆö typos in user config should fail loudly at load, not silently leave entries un-quarantined forever.

The registry is cached at module level (`_REGISTRY_CACHE`); call `reset_for_tests()` between tests if you monkeypatch config. The pool's `_compute_expires_at` soft-imports this module to avoid an import cycle (sources imports background for type hints).

Why not put this in `triage/config.py`: that file holds stage-shape config (profile names, max_tokens, escalation chain) ŌĆö knobs about HOW the LLM call runs. This file holds lifecycle config (TTLs, quarantine triggers) ŌĆö knobs about how entries AGE in the pool. Different concerns; keep them split.

triage/pool-entry-lifecycle.name: PoolEntry Lifecycle
triage/pool-entry-lifecycle.description: PoolEntry lifecycle in the triage pool ŌĆö state enum, expires_at, sweep transitions

content.full:
# ClarifyEntry / PoolEntry Lifecycle

Every entry in the Clarify pool (formerly triage pool) carries a `state` enum describing where it sits in the review lifecycle. Slice 1 added this; before Slice 1, entries had only a `reviewed_at` timestamp (None or stamped) and no explicit lifecycle marker.

Slice 3 renamed the dataclass `PoolEntry ŌåÆ ClarifyEntry` and the pool `TriagePool ŌåÆ ClarifyPool`. Module-level aliases preserve the legacy names so on-disk pool snapshots (which name the class in their dataclass repr) and external callers still resolve.

## States

| State | Meaning | Visible in Resolution Surface? | Set by |
|---|---|---|---|
| `pending` | Waiting for human review (default at creation) | Yes | `ClarifyPool.submit` / `submit_raw` |
| `stale` | Past `expires_at` per source descriptor's TTL. Soft signal, non-destructive | No | `triage_pool_sweep` (daily) |
| `quarantined` | The source no longer resolves (file deleted, journal text drifted, capture-tag removed) OR the user re-directed the card via the Resolution Surface. `quarantine_reason` records which trigger fired | No | `triage_pool_sweep` (daily) and `dashboard.resolution_redirect` (Slice 1.5) |
| `reviewed` | Human acted on it via the dashboard's Resolution Surface | No | `ClarifyPool.mark_reviewed` (when `dashboard.review_submit` succeeds) |
| `dropped` | Explicitly removed (rare; reserved for manual intervention) | No | not yet wired |

Quarantine takes precedence over stale: if an entry is both expired AND has a quarantine trigger firing, the sweep marks it quarantined (more specific signal ŌĆö the source is GONE, not just OLD).

## Lifecycle fields on ClarifyEntry

```python
@dataclass
class ClarifyEntry:
    # ... pre-Slice-1 fields (run_id, adapter, source, item_id, item, verdict, created_at, reviewed_at, review_outcome, item_content_hash) ...
    state: str = STATE_PENDING                  # canonical lifecycle marker
    expires_at: str | None = None               # ISO8601, set from descriptor TTL
    quarantine_reason: str | None = None        # e.g. "source_removed", "user_redirected"
    state_changed_at: str | None = None         # ISO8601, stamped on every transition
    # Slice 1.5 -----------------------------------------------------------
    attraction_passes: int = 0                  # bumped by the Resolution Surface Later button
    forced_context: dict | None = None          # captured by Resolution Surface Re-direct
```

`expires_at` is computed at creation time from the source descriptor's `ttl_days` (see `triage/source-descriptors`). `None` means no auto-expiry ŌĆö inline captures use this because they're user-affirmative.

`attraction_passes` is the user-driven defer counter (Slice 1.5). Each Resolution-Surface Later click bumps it by one. Slice 8 reads this for resurfacing priority ŌĆö it does NOT change `state` (a deferred entry stays `pending` so it surfaces again on the next render). The signal is non-shaming by design; the surface never moralizes about high counts.

`forced_context` is the user-driven re-direct payload (Slice 1.5). When the user picks Re-direct, the Resolution Surface persists their freeform/structured correction here AND quarantines the current entry (`quarantine_reason="user_redirected"`). Slice 3's Clarify rewrite reads `forced_context` and re-runs the relevant pipeline step with that context as a forced input.

## Slice 3 verdict shapes (relevant to lifecycle)

The `verdict` field on each entry can carry three different shapes:

- **Legacy single-action** (Slice 1): `{recommended_action: 'create_task'|..., rationale, group_intent, ...}`.
- **Raw** (Slice 1, when verdict-pass gated off): `{raw: True}`.
- **Multi-record** (Slice 3, when verdict-pass enabled): `{rationale, group_intent, records: [...], refusal: {...} | None, pipeline_blocker: ...}`.

The presentation builder branches read on this shape; lifecycle transitions are independent of the verdict shape ŌĆö a `pending` entry stays `pending` regardless of which schema its verdict uses, and `mark_reviewed` works the same way for all three.

## Backwards compatibility

Legacy entries (pre-Slice-1) lack a `state` field on disk. `ClarifyEntry.from_dict` infers it from `reviewed_at`: stamped ŌåÆ `reviewed`, None ŌåÆ `pending`. So existing pool snapshots load without a migration; the field gets persisted on the next mutation through `ClarifyPool.submit` / `mark_reviewed` / etc.

Slice 1.5's two new fields default to `0` and `None` respectively, so legacy entries also load without a migration.

Slice 3 entries with the new multi-record verdict shape coexist with legacy verdicts in the same pool; the presentation builder picks the right rendering branch via `derive_resolution_type` + the `is_multi_record` discriminator.

## How transitions fire

- `pending ŌåÆ stale`: daily sweep, `expires_at < now`.
- `pending ŌåÆ quarantined`: daily sweep (source descriptor's quarantine triggers fire) OR `POST /api/triage/redirect` (user re-directed).
- `pending ŌåÆ reviewed`: user submits a decision via the dashboard's Resolution Surface, **and** the executor reports the operation succeeded. Failed ops keep the entry pending so the user can retry. Slice 3 multi-record entries follow the same path; the executor's success buckets (`tasks_created`, `deleted`, `references_logged`, `calendar_logged`, `records_executed`) all feed the success-filter.
- `pending ŌåÆ pending` with `attraction_passes += 1`: user clicked Later via the Resolution Surface (no state change; just a counter bump).
- `* ŌåÆ dropped`: not wired in Slice 1; reserved for future explicit-removal flows.

## How to query

```python
from work_buddy.clarify.background import get_pool, STATE_QUARANTINED
# or, equivalently, from work_buddy.triage.background (legacy shim).

pool = get_pool()
pending = pool.pending()                                      # state == "pending" + legacy
quarantined = pool.entries_in_state(STATE_QUARANTINED)        # exact match
all_entries = pool.all_entries()                              # any state
```

`pool.pending()` is what the Resolution Surface uses; it filters to `state == pending` AND falls back to the legacy `reviewed_at IS NULL` check for entries that predate the field.

## What user-facing surfaces show

The Resolution Surface shows ONLY pending entries (Slice 1 default). Stale and quarantined entries persist on disk for audit but are hidden from the active surface. The user can inspect them via `pool.entries_in_state(...)` from a script.

triage/pool-entry-lifecycle.dev_notes:
## State enum constants.

`STATE_PENDING`, `STATE_STALE`, `STATE_QUARANTINED`, `STATE_REVIEWED`, `STATE_DROPPED` and the `POOL_ENTRY_STATES` frozenset are exported from `work_buddy.triage.background`. Always import the constants, never hardcode the strings.

## `mark_state` vs `mark_reviewed`.

The reviewed transition stamps `reviewed_at` + `review_outcome` AND sets `state=reviewed`; the generic `mark_state` does NOT touch reviewed_at. Calling `mark_state(..., state=STATE_REVIEWED)` raises ValueError on purpose ŌĆö the path is `mark_reviewed`.

## Idempotence.

`mark_state` is no-op if the entry is already in the target state (won't double-stamp `state_changed_at`). `increment_attraction_pass`, by contrast, ALWAYS bumps ŌĆö each call is one defer event, even if the entry was deferred before. Don't try to make defer idempotent; the count is the point.

## Backwards-compat read path.

`PoolEntry.from_dict` infers state from reviewed_at; this means **never write a state field with a None value to disk**, or you'll defeat the inference for legacy readers. The dataclass default makes this hard to do accidentally. Slice 1.5's `attraction_passes` (default 0) and `forced_context` (default None) are also written through the dataclass; legacy entries pick them up on their next mutation.

## Stale state-changed-at on legacy entries.

Existing reviewed entries got `state=reviewed` inferred but their `state_changed_at` was unset until the migration script (`scripts/migrate_pool_slice_1.py`) backfilled it from `reviewed_at`. After the migration, all entries have a `state_changed_at`; new entries get one stamped on creation.

## Adding a new state value.

Append to the enum, add to `POOL_ENTRY_STATES`, decide the transition rules, update `mark_state` validation. Don't repurpose existing values ŌĆö `from_dict`'s legacy inference would break.

## The `dropped` state is reserved.

Slice 1 doesn't wire a transition into it. Future use case: explicit user action "this entry should never come back" (vs stale which can theoretically be revived if conditions change). Until that wires up, the constant exists but no code writes to it.

## Resolution Surface defer never changes state.

The Later button bumps `attraction_passes` only; the entry stays `pending`. Resist the temptation to add a `deferred` state ŌĆö Slice 8's resurfacing system reads the counter, not a state, so the entry needs to stay surfaceable. The counter does the disambiguation.

## Re-direct uses the existing quarantine path.

`POST /api/triage/redirect` calls `mark_state(..., state=STATE_QUARANTINED, reason="user_redirected")` ŌĆö same code path as the daily sweep's source-removed quarantine. The `quarantine_reason` string is free-form metadata (not a registered enum); `"user_redirected"` is just a discriminator readers can branch on if they care.

triage/card-actions.name: Triage card actions (open-in-app seam)
triage/card-actions.description: Declarative per-source button on Review cards that dispatches an MCP capability via /api/palette/execute, with auto-quarantine on declared error_kinds

content.full:
# Card actions on triage Review cards

Most triage sources need a per-source "open in app" affordance ŌĆö open the
email in Thunderbird, focus the Chrome tab, jump to the journal note. Hard-
coding these in the dashboard frontend would require a JS dispatch table
that grows for every new source. The card-actions seam keeps the frontend
generic and pushes per-source knowledge into the existing source-descriptor
registry.

## Shape (declarative, lives in `SourceDescriptor.config`)

```python
"open_action": {
    "label": "Open in Thunderbird",
    "capability": "email_display",
    "param_map": {
        "provider_message_id": "metadata.provider_message_id",
        "folder_path":         "metadata.folder_path",
    },
    "quarantine_on_error_kinds": ["email_message_not_found"],
}
```

- `label` ŌĆö visible button text.
- `capability` ŌĆö MCP capability name. Frontend dispatches via
  `command_id="work-buddy::<capability>"` to the existing
  `POST /api/palette/execute` endpoint. No new HTTP surface.
- `param_map` ŌĆö dot-paths into the item dict (`TriageItem.to_dict()` shape)
  resolving to the capability's kwargs. `metadata.<key>` is the common case.
- `quarantine_on_error_kinds` (optional) ŌĆö when the action fails with one of
  these `error_kind` values, the dashboard auto-fires
  `triage_pool_quarantine_entry`. Self-healing UX: the click failure becomes
  the evidence for quarantine without waiting for the cron sweep.

## Resolution flow

1. `_build_presentation_from_pool` (in `triage_review_pool.py`) iterates
   pending pool entries.
2. For each item it calls `card_actions.build_card_actions(source, item)`,
   which looks up the descriptor and resolves `param_map` against the item.
3. If all required params resolve to non-empty values, an action dict
   lands on `modal_item['actions']`. If any param is missing or empty, the
   action is silently dropped ŌĆö better an absent button than a broken click.
4. The presentation builder also drops the legacy `url` field on items
   whose source has `open_action` (handles dead `thunderbird:msg/<key>`
   synthetic links on pre-Slice-2 pool data; chrome_tab still keeps its
   real https:// URL because chrome_tab has no open_action declaration).

## What the frontend receives

```js
item.actions = [{
    label: "Open in Thunderbird",
    command_id: "work-buddy::email_display",
    params: { provider_message_id: "...", folder_path: "..." },
    quarantine_on_error_kinds: ["email_message_not_found"],
}]
```

The frontend has zero per-source knowledge. It iterates the array, renders
one button per entry, and on click POSTs to `/api/palette/execute`. On
failure it extracts `error_kind` from the wrapped capability response and
checks against `quarantine_on_error_kinds` ŌĆö if it matches, fires
`triage_pool_quarantine_entry` with `(group.pool_run_id, item.id,
reason="source_removed")` and visually fades the row.

Shared helpers (`window._wvExecuteAction`, `_wvExtractErrorKind`,
`_wvQuarantineEntry`) live in `script_triage.py` and are reused by the
right-side drawer in `script_review.py`.

## Defensive properties

The seam fails closed at every layer:

- **Resolver** (`build_card_actions`) returns `[]` on:
  empty source, no descriptor, no `open_action` block, missing
  `capability`/`label`, non-string keys/values in `param_map`,
  missing or empty resolved param.
- **Frontend** (`_wvQuarantineEntry`) requires both `pool_run_id` and
  `item.id`; missing either surfaces the original error verbatim instead
  of silently failing.
- **Quarantine capability** (`triage_pool_quarantine_entry`) is
  idempotent. Re-running on an already-quarantined entry returns
  `stamped: 0` rather than erroring.

## Adding a new action source

1. Pick a friendly key in `KNOWN_VERDICT_PASS_SOURCES` or whatever
   maps it to `TriageItem.source`. (The card-action key is just the
   source name; no separate registration.)
2. Declare `open_action` in the source descriptor's `config` block in
   `triage/sources.py`.
3. (Optional) declare `quarantine_on_error_kinds` listing the capability's
   error_kind strings that mean "the source is gone" ŌĆö frontend will
   auto-quarantine on those.
4. The capability you reference must already be registered in
   `mcp_server/registry.py` and kwarg-compatible with the resolved
   `params`.

That's it. No frontend changes, no new HTTP endpoint, no new dispatch
table. Tests in `tests/unit/test_card_actions.py` will catch missing
fields and malformed declarations at fixture build time.

## Currently shipped

| Source            | Action label         | Capability       | Auto-quarantine on |
|-------------------|----------------------|------------------|--------------------|
| `email_message`   | Open in Thunderbird  | `email_display`  | `email_message_not_found` |

Reserved keys for future capabilities: `chrome_tab` (focus tab in Chrome,
already partially exists via the chrome native host), `journal_thread`
(jump to journal note in Obsidian), `inline` (jump to source line).

triage/card-actions.dev_notes: **Don't add a JS-side per-source dispatch table.** The whole point of this seam is keeping the frontend generic. New behavior goes in the descriptor, gets resolved in the presentation builder, and reaches the frontend as data. **Don't bypass the resolver to inject actions directly into items.** The defensive guards in `build_card_actions` catch malformed param_maps and missing metadata; bypassing them ships brittle clicks. **Don't quarantine on click failure without a declared error_kind allowlist.** A bridge timeout shouldn't quarantine ŌĆö only signals that prove the source is gone (the capability returned `email_message_not_found`, not `email_bridge_unreachable`). Empty `quarantine_on_error_kinds` is the safe default.

triage/resolution-surface.name: Resolution Surface (Slice 1.5)
triage/resolution-surface.description: Type-aware decorator over the Review-tab card renderer; adds typed pipeline-blocker badges, attraction-passes display, Defer + Re-direct affordances, and a j/k/enter/r/s/? keyboard layer. Dispatches per resolution_type. Reads multi-record + refusal verdicts (Slice 3).

content.full:
# Resolution Surface

A unified, type-aware surface over the dashboard Review tab for user-driven resolution of agent uncertainty. Implements ROADMAP ┬¦3.5 (Edit vs Re-direct interaction modes) and ┬¦3.3 (typed pipeline blockers) on top of the existing per-card review flow.

Not a replacement for `renderTriageReview` ŌĆö a decorator over it. Each card keeps its existing edit affordances (action pills, drag-to-reassign, namespace tags, per-source open buttons, per-group submit) and gains:

- A typed **pipeline-blocker badge** explaining *why* the agent stopped (per ROADMAP ┬¦3.3).
- An **attraction-passes count** showing how many times the user has deferred the card. Slice 8 reads this for resurfacing priority.
- A **Defer** button + `s` keybinding ŌĆö non-shaming "Later" path; bumps `attraction_passes` without acting on the card.
- A **Re-direct** button + `r` keybinding ŌĆö opens a free-form text input; on submit, persists `forced_context` on the entry and quarantines the entry. Slice 3's Clarify rewrite reads `forced_context` and re-runs the relevant pipeline step.
- A **keyboard layer** ŌĆö `k`/`j` move focus between cards (k=down, j=up; user-preference inversion of vim defaults), `enter` fires the focused card's per-group submit, `s` defers, `r` revises, `?` toggles a help overlay. Skips when an input/textarea/select has focus or any modifier key is held (so Ctrl+K command palette and tab-switch shortcuts pass through).

## Resolution-type taxonomy

Declared in `work_buddy/clarify/resolution.py` as `RESOLUTION_TYPES`. Backend stamps a `resolution_type` field on every presentation_group; frontend dispatches per-type renderers off it.

| Type | Status | Owning slice |
|---|---|---|
| `verdict_review` | live | Slices 1+1.5+3 |
| `raw_capture` | live (verdict-pass-disabled entries) | Slices 1+1.5 |
| `clarification` | **live (Slice 3)** | Slice 3 ŌĆö fires when verdict carries a non-empty `refusal` field |
| `placement` | placeholder | Slice 6 |
| `decomposition` | placeholder | Slice 7 |
| `plan_approval` | placeholder | Slice 4 |
| `output_review` | placeholder | Slice 4 |

Forward-compat types render a one-line note explaining which slice will activate them, instead of the live edit affordances. Adding the strings now means downstream slices add data, not taxonomies.

## Pipeline-blocker taxonomy (ROADMAP ┬¦3.3)

Declared in `work_buddy/clarify/resolution.py` as `PIPELINE_BLOCKERS`. The backend reads `verdict.pipeline_blocker` (string or `{kind, detail}` dict) and resolves it through `extract_pipeline_blocker(verdict)`, which returns a presentation dict the frontend renders as a typed badge with optional deep-link affordance.

| Kind | Tone | Deep-link |
|---|---|---|
| `agent_context_unmet` | blocked | `/setup` ("Open setup wizard") |
| `user_context_unmet` | deferred | ŌĆö |
| `consent_required` | blocked | ŌĆö |
| `clarification_required` | info | ŌĆö |
| `risk_threshold_exceeded` | blocked | ŌĆö |
| `inference_uncertain` | info | ŌĆö |

Unknown kinds are dropped silently ŌĆö better no badge than a misleading one.

## Backend: persistence

Two Slice 1.5 fields on `ClarifyEntry` (formerly `PoolEntry`; see `triage/pool-entry-lifecycle`):

- `attraction_passes: int = 0` ŌĆö bumped each time the user clicks Later. Slice 8 reads this for resurfacing priority.
- `forced_context: dict | None = None` ŌĆö captured by Re-direct mode. Slice 3 reads this when re-running the pipeline step with the user's correction as a forced input.

Two helper methods on `ClarifyPool`:

- `increment_attraction_pass(entry_keys)` ŌĆö bumps the counter on each entry. Returns the number actually mutated.
- `store_forced_context(run_id, item_id, forced_context)` ŌĆö persists a Re-direct payload. Returns True on success, False if the entry was not found.

Verdict-shape allowlist (in `_shape_verdict`) accepts both legacy single-action fields AND Slice 3 multi-record fields (records, refusal, pipeline_blocker).

## Backend: HTTP surface

Two endpoints on the dashboard, both wrapping `work_buddy.consent.user_initiated` so nested `@requires_consent` gates pass through with `USER_INITIATED` audit-log entries (clicks ARE the consent ŌĆö see `notifications/consent`):

- `POST /api/triage/defer` ŌåÆ `{pool_run_id, item_id}` ŌåÆ `{status, bumped, attraction_passes}`. Bumps the counter; does NOT mark the entry reviewed ("not now" ŌēĀ "acted on").
- `POST /api/triage/redirect` ŌåÆ `{pool_run_id, item_id, forced_context, target_step?}` ŌåÆ `{status, stored, quarantined, target_step}`. Persists `forced_context` (with `target_step` defaulted to `"clarify"`) and quarantines the entry with `quarantine_reason="user_redirected"`. Empty/whitespace-only forced_context is rejected (400) ŌĆö there'd be no signal for Slice 3 to re-run on.

Both gate via `_reject_read_only()` so read-only deployments stay read-only. URL paths kept as `/api/triage/*` even after the rename ŌĆö they're public API, no semantic gain in renaming.

## Backend: presentation builder

`_build_presentation_from_pool` (in `work_buddy/clarify/capabilities/triage_review_pool.py`) stamps these fields on every presentation_group:

- `resolution_type` ŌĆö derived via `derive_resolution_type(pe.verdict)`. Slice 3: refusal-bearing verdicts resolve to `clarification` (Slice 1.5 added `verdict_review` / `raw_capture`).
- `pipeline_blocker` ŌĆö derived via `extract_pipeline_blocker(pe.verdict)`. `None` when the verdict didn't declare one.
- `attraction_passes` ŌĆö int; defaults to 0 for entries that predate the field.
- `records` ŌĆö Slice 3 multi-record verdict array (or None for legacy / raw entries).
- `refusal` ŌĆö Slice 3 refusal payload (or None).
- `is_multi_record` ŌĆö boolean discriminator.

And one field on every modal_item: `pool_run_id` ŌĆö so the frontend can target a specific entry for Defer/Re-direct without rummaging through `items`.

## Frontend

`work_buddy/dashboard/frontend/script_resolution.py` defines `mountResolutionSurface(container, presentation, options)` and bundles its own CSS via `_resolution_surface_styles()`.

The Review tab calls `window.mountResolutionSurface(container, presentation, {...})` instead of the bare `renderTriageReview(...)`. The mount delegates to `renderTriageReview` for the per-card edit flow but installs a `decorateCard` callback that adds the Slice 1.5 affordances (idempotent across `rerenderCard` re-renders).

Script ordering matters: `_resolution_surface_script()` MUST be spliced after `_triage_review_script()` in `frontend/__init__.py` because the decorator calls `renderTriageReview`.

## Slice 1 patterns preserved

All six load-bearing patterns from Slice 1 still hold:

1. `user_initiated` consent context wraps `/api/review/execute` AND the two new endpoints.
2. Success-only `mark_reviewed` filter ŌĆö unchanged in `/api/review/execute`. Defer + Re-direct don't touch `mark_reviewed`. Slice 3 buckets (deleted, references_logged, calendar_logged, records_executed) are added to the filter so multi-record entries also stamp reviewed when their executor runs.
3. `operation_errors` surfacing in `/api/review/execute` ŌĆö unchanged.
4. Raw-entry rendering branch ŌĆö `is_raw` still flows; `resolution_type=raw_capture` is the parallel signal.
5. `perGroupSubmit: true` ŌĆö Resolution Surface mounts with this; `enter` keybinding fires the per-card submit, not a batch.
6. `ObsidianPostWriteUncertain` retry affordance ŌĆö unchanged (out-of-scope for Slice 1.5; follow-up work).

## Slice 3 multi-record execution

When a presentation_group carries `records: [...]`, the executor splits it off from the legacy action ops and routes per record (`_execute_multi_record_decisions` ŌåÆ `_execute_record` ŌåÆ `_execute_record_task` for task destinations). User-level overrides apply at the group level: `action='leave'` skips records; `action='close'` treats the whole group as a coarse delete. See `clarify/multi-record-schema` for the schema and `clarify` for the full pipeline.

## Non-goals (deferred to follow-up tasks)

- Multi-select project dropdown with top-N candidates + confidence indicators (depends on Slice 4 risk_profile + Slice 5b namespace candidate scoring).
- Batch decision pattern ("apply same to all 5 similar items").
- Urgency differentiation (alert pinned to top with red styling, digest collapsible at bottom) ŌĆö depends on Slice 4's `resurfacing_level`.
- Real per-resolution-type renderers for placement / decomposition / plan_approval / output_review ŌĆö depends on Slices 4, 6, 7. Until then they render a one-line placeholder explaining which slice activates them.

triage/resolution-surface.dev_notes:
## decorateCard hook

Lives on `renderTriageReview(options.decorateCard)`. Default is a no-op so the Chrome triage modal and tests behave as before. Invoked at the end of `renderGroupCard` and (by extension) on every targeted re-render via `rerenderCard`. Wrap your decoration in a try/catch when the callback could throw; the renderer logs and continues so a buggy decorator doesn't tear down the whole card grid.

## Idempotence on re-render

The Slice 1.5 decoration is idempotent: each call removes any pre-existing `.wv-blocker-badge`, `.wv-pass-count`, `.wv-resolution-placeholder`, and `.wv-res-actions` on the card before re-adding them. Don't move state into the decorator; pull it from the `group` argument every time.

## Keyboard layer install

`_resInstallKeyboardLayer(container)` attaches a `keydown` listener at `document` scope (not container) so cards still respond when focus is on body. Guarded by `container._wvResKeyboardInstalled` so re-mounts don't double-install. The listener early-returns when `document.activeElement` is INPUT/TEXTAREA/SELECT or when any modifier key is held (preserves Ctrl+K command palette, tab-switch shortcuts, and form typing).

## Why decorator over replacement

Building a true per-resolution-type dispatcher (one renderer per type) is the long-term goal but premature now: only verdict_review and raw_capture have real data, and the existing `renderTriageReview` solves the per-card edit flow well. The decorator buys the user-visible Slice 1.5 affordances today without forking a renderer that Slices 3+ will replace anyway.

## Re-direct quarantine reason

`quarantine_reason="user_redirected"` is a new value but `mark_state` accepts arbitrary reason strings (the field is free-form metadata, not a registered enum). Source descriptors don't need updating; the daily sweep ignores entries already in `quarantined` state.

## Empty forced_context rejection

`/api/triage/redirect` rejects 400 when `forced_context` has no truthy values (after stripping strings). Without that, a Re-direct click with an empty form would just be a fancy quarantine ŌĆö no signal for Slice 3 to re-run on. Free text or any structured field qualifies.

## Why `pool_run_id` lands on items, not just groups

The presentation has `pool_run_id` on each `presentation_group`, but Defer + Re-direct take an `(item_id, run_id)` pair. Surfacing `pool_run_id` on `modal_item` too means the frontend doesn't have to walk back from the item to its enclosing group; the Slice 1.5 fetch payload is `{pool_run_id: item.pool_run_id, item_id: item.id}`. Trade-off: ~32 bytes of duplication per item; saves a DOM walk on every keypress.
